body.p:
Sometimes XPath is no longer sufficient for searching the XML tree, and you want to apply regular expressions to text nodes. I know of three XPath functions in which regular expressions can be specified:
UWE test footnote (25/40, callout Y): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
When entering the regular expression, you naturally have to escape characters such as >,<,&, which makes the expression even a bit more complicated compared to conventional non-XML programming.
The expression that matches all XML tags, for example, is the following: <[>!]> Here, the characters reserved for XML are already escaped.

section.title: XSLT Analyze String
section.p: But there is also a dedicated XSLT construct, independent of XPath, for applying regular expressions. The source code for it looks like this:

Here, every text node of an element is examined, and if it contains an XML tag, it is wrapped in a <b> tag via the xsl:matching-substring instruction. In the next transformation step, this is then rendered in bold.
This is a simple syntax highlighter for XML source code. The syntax highlighter for this PDF is implemented in this way.

note.p: You can also nest xsl:analyze-string elements within xsl:matching-substring and xsl:non-matching-substring elements, which naturally allows for much more complex problem solving.
